Always cleanup temporary files, logs.
Never commit or store sensitive data, credentials, or personally identifiable information in the repository. Check before commiting.
Use make_deploy script to build binaries and upload to test server
For the Web-Ui there is a help text file. Keep it up to date.
Do not forget to update tool manuals and prompts if you add new integrations oder tools for the agent.
Security by design: Always consider security implications when adding new tools, integrations, or code. Avoid introducing vulnerabilities or exposing sensitive data.
New tools and integrations that can potenially change or delete data or perform critical operations should habe a read only toggle.
If the tool or integration needs more granular permissions to be usefull, use separate toggles for read ,write, change and delete access. 
all tools and integrations should have a toggle to activate them unless they are essential for the system to fuction properly.
Do all Web UI work as user friendly as possible. Avoid too technical jargon and provide clear instructions and feedback to the user. Do not break the style of the UI. Your changes should be a masterpiece of UX design and fit seamlessly into the existing interface. If you see bad UX in the existing UI feel free to improve it as long as you keep the overall style and design consistent.
If anything passes external content to the agent implement the necessary safety measures to prevent prompt injection and other attacks. Always assume that external content is potentially malicious and treat it accordingly. Use the `<external_data>` wrapper for all untrusted content and never allow it to influence agent behavior or tool calls directly.
always store credentials and sensitive data directly in the secrets vault, never in the code or configuration files.
When doing web UI work do not forget to update the translation files in ui/lang for ALL supported languages. Always keep the translations up to date and consistent with the changes you make in the UI. If you add new features or tools that require new UI elements, make sure to provide translations for all supported languages.
If files start to get too big and unwieldy, split them into smaller, more manageable pieces. Orient yourself on what an ai agent can handle with ease. If a file becomes too large for the agent to process effectively, consider breaking it down into smaller files that are easier to manage and understand. Always aim for clarity and maintainability in your file structure.
Implement tests for critical functionality and new features. This includes unit tests for individual functions and integration tests for tools and workflows. Tests help ensure that your code works as expected and can prevent regressions in the future. Aim for good test coverage, especially for complex logic and critical operations.
If you change database schemas, always implement a migration strategy to handle existing data. This may involve writing migration scripts or using a migration tool to update the database schema without losing data. Always backup the database before performing migrations and test the migration process in a staging environment before applying it to production.
The system you design should always offer the user a way to disable tools that could be potentially harmfull via the UI. If a tool has the potential to cause harm or perform critical operations, it should not be enabled by default and should have a clear toggle in the UI to allow users to enable or disable it as needed. Always prioritize user safety and security when designing and implementing tools and features for the agent. The agent should normaly not have access to passwords, tokena, or any other sensitive data. If a tool requires credentials to function, it should be designed to retrieve those credentials securely from the vault at runtime, rather than storing them in the code or configuration files. The only exception is if the user gives those data to the agent and he stores them in the vault for later use. 
The config.yaml file is holy. No update should be made to it without careful consideration of the implications and potential consequences.
The update, and install scripts and the Dockerfiles need to be kept up to date and consistent with the changes you make in the system. If you add new tools, integrations, or features that require changes to the installation or update process, make sure to update the relevant scripts and Dockerfiles accordingly.
